title: Marketplaces
subtitle: The official catalog, the community one with review, and the /plugin command: how to choose and install

theory.md:
A plugin **marketplace** is not a store with a storefront and payment, but a catalog: a repository with a list of plugins and information on where to install them from. There are two main catalogs:

- **The official one** — `anthropics/claude-plugins-official`, an Anthropic-curated marketplace of ~100 plugins: integrations with GitHub, Linear, Notion, tools for AWS, Cloudflare, MongoDB, and much more. It's connected in Claude Code out of the box.
- **The community one** — `anthropics/claude-plugins-community`: community plugins that enter the catalog through a review process — submissions are examined before acceptance.

And a third level: **any Git repository can become a marketplace** with a catalog file inside. This is how teams make internal marketplaces: one repository — and the whole company shares a set of signature plugins.

Why a catalog at all, if a plugin can be downloaded from anywhere? For the same reason a phone has an app store: a single place to search, standard installation, and updates. Install a plugin from a marketplace — and when the author releases a new version, you update the normal way, rather than "download the folder again and lay it out by hand."

All plugin management is gathered into one slash command — **`/plugin`**. It opens an interactive menu: browsing catalogs, searching, installing, enabling and disabling, removing. The main moves:

- **View and install**: `/plugin` → pick a marketplace → pick a plugin → install. Before installing, the menu shows which components will arrive: how many commands, whether there are skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers — read this list carefully, it matters more than the marketing description.
- **Connect a new marketplace**: `/plugin marketplace add owner/repo` — for example, the community catalog or your team's internal repository.
- **Manage what's installed**: plugins can be temporarily disabled without removing them — handy for debugging, when you need to figure out which plugin is interfering.

After installing, check the result with the same commands as always: `/help` shows the plugin's new slash commands, `/mcp` — its MCP servers. If something is missing — look at the plugin's description: it may need environment variables or an access token.

theory.title: A typical plugin session
theory.code:
> /plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-community
  ✔ Marketplace connected: claude-plugins-community

> /plugin
  Marketplaces: claude-plugins-official, claude-plugins-community
  → Browse plugins → github (official)
    Components: 3 commands, 1 MCP server
  → Install

  ✔ Installed the github plugin. New commands visible in /help

theory.text: How to choose: trust matters more than features

theory.md:
A plugin is not passive text. Hooks run commands on your machine automatically, on session events; MCP servers are runnable code with access wherever you let it; skills and commands directly affect the agent's behavior across all your projects. So installing a plugin is a trust decision, like installing a program on your computer, and before install it's worth going through a checklist:

1. **Who's the author?** The official catalog is curated; the community one passes review; a random repository — only your own inspection.
2. **Which components get installed?** A plugin of commands alone is more harmless than one with hooks and MCP servers: the latter execute code.
3. **What's inside?** A catalog is a Git repository: any plugin's source can be opened and read before installing.
4. **Is the project alive?** The date of the last update and open issues say no less about quality than the description.

This checklist takes two minutes, and it saves you from the main troubles: someone else's code with excess permissions and "dead" plugins that break on the next Claude Code update.

rows: High, but reading the description is still worth it
Medium: there was review, but check the components

theory.bad: Saw a "top-10 plugins for Claude Code" on social media, added an unfamiliar marketplace, and installed all ten at once. A week later sessions are sluggish, the agent behaves oddly, and which plugin is to blame is unknown.
theory.good: Found the needed integration in the official catalog via /plugin, looked at the component list (2 commands, 1 MCP server), installed one plugin, worked with it for a day, confirmed its usefulness — and only then added the next one.
theory.explanation: Plugins are installed one at a time and deliberately: each adds commands, skills, and servers to every session of yours. Ten unverified plugins at once is both a risk (hooks and servers execute code) and undebuggable: when a problem arises you'll have to disable them one by one. Going gradually gives both safety and an understanding of what actually brings value.

theory.md: The Claude Code documentation (Discover plugins / Plugin marketplaces) describes /plugin as a single management point: browsing catalogs, installing and disabling plugins, connecting marketplaces with the `/plugin marketplace add` command. A Git repository with a plugin catalog can act as a marketplace; Anthropic's official marketplace is available out of the box.

A plugin with hooks gets the right to run commands on your machine automatically, on session events. This is the most powerful — and the riskiest — component. See hooks/ in an unfamiliar plugin — be sure to read which commands it runs before installing.

A great way to learn is to read other people's plugins from the official catalog: it's a GitHub repository where you can see how the pros build manifests, commands, and servers. Find a plugin similar to your idea and peek at its structure before the world's final boss.

tasks.instruction: Connect the community marketplace with the command `/plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-community`, then open the plugins menu with the `/plugin` command.

steps.output:
✔ Marketplace connected: claude-plugins-community
  Source: github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-community
  Plugins in catalog: 47
steps.failHint: A marketplace is connected like this: /plugin marketplace add anthropics/claude-plugins-community

steps.output:
Plugins

  Marketplaces:
  • claude-plugins-official (default) — ~100 plugins
  • claude-plugins-community — 47 plugins

  [ Browse plugins ]  [ Installed ]  [ Marketplaces ]

  Select a plugin to see its components and install.
steps.failHint: The plugin management menu is opened by the /plugin slash command

tasks.successMessage: Now you have access to both main catalogs. Note: before installing any plugin, the menu shows its components — that's your security checkpoint.

tasks.question: How does the official claude-plugins-official marketplace differ from the community catalog?
options: The official one is paid, the community one is free
The official one is curated by Anthropic; in community, community plugins enter through submission review
The official one works only on macOS
In no way — they're mirrors of the same repository
tasks.explanation: The difference is in the curation model: the official catalog is assembled and maintained by Anthropic (~100 plugins: github, linear, notion, aws, and others), while community is filled with community plugins through a review process. Both are free, both work everywhere, and they're different repositories with different contents.
tasks.hint: Think not about money and platforms, but about who verifies the catalog's contents.

tasks.question: What should you check BEFORE installing an unfamiliar plugin? Choose ALL the correct items:
options: Who the author is and which marketplace the plugin comes from
Which components it installs — especially whether there are hooks and MCP servers
The source in the catalog's repository, if the source is unfamiliar
The number of emojis in the description
Nothing: once a plugin made it into some catalog, it's safe
tasks.explanation: Three checks from the lesson's checklist: the source and author, the makeup of the components (hooks and servers execute code — pay them special attention), and, when in doubt, reading the source. Emojis say nothing about safety, and blind trust in any catalog is a mistake: catalogs have different levels of verification.
tasks.hint: There are three correct answers, and all of them are about trust and makeup, not about presentation.

tasks.instruction: A colleague's notes on marketplaces. One statement is dangerously wrong — find it.
lines: Anthropic's official marketplace is available in Claude Code out of the box.
A new marketplace is connected with the /plugin marketplace add owner/repo command.
Plugins from the community catalog need not be checked — review guarantees complete safety.
A team's internal marketplace is an ordinary Git repository with a plugin catalog.
An installed plugin can be temporarily disabled without removing it.
tasks.explanation: Review in the community catalog is a filter, not a guarantee: it reduces risk but doesn't cancel your own check. Before installing, still look at the author and the components — especially hooks and MCP servers, which execute code on your machine. The other statements are correct.

tasks.instruction:
Mission: assemble your own set of plugins. **Requires Claude Code.**

1. Run `claude` and open `/plugin`. Browse the official catalog: find 2-3 plugins useful specifically to you (integration with your tracker? your cloud? your git host?).
2. For each candidate, open the description and look at the component makeup: how many commands, whether there are skills, hooks, MCP servers.
3. Install one plugin that passed your checklist. Check `/help` — did its commands appear; if there's an MCP server — check `/mcp`.
4. Try the plugin on a real task. If it didn't come in handy — disable or remove it via /plugin: hygiene matters no less than installation.
checklist: I browsed the official catalog and found plugins for my tasks
Before installing, I checked the candidate's component makeup
The plugin is installed, its commands are visible in /help
I applied the plugin to a real task and decided whether to keep it
